coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/noble metal co-decorated MIL-53(Fe) as difunctional photocatalysts for the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and dyes
coredata.dc:creator: He, Zhoujun
Liang, Ruowen
Zhou, Chen
Yan, Guiyang
Wu, Ling
coredata.dc:description: Industrial sewage often contain metal ions and dyes, and it is difficult to efficiently remove them simultaneously. In this work, novel ternary MIL-53(Fe)/carbon quantum dots/noble metal (MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs, MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) nanocomposites have been rationally constructed and successfully fabricated by a facile two-step strategy with self-assembling and in-situ photoreduction. The resulting MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au composite exhibits excellent photoactivity for the Cr(VI) reduction, the reduction ratio is nearly 100% after 20 min of visible light illumination. The rate constant of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au is calculated to be 0.1820 min−1, which is around 7.4, 2.76, 1.77 and 1.47 times higher than that of as-prepared MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd, respectively. More significantly, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au nanocomposite has been proven to serve as bifunctional photocatalyst for simultaneously removing Cr(VI)/dye mixed system pollutants. The superior photoactivity may be due to the enhanced light absorption intensity, the superior electrical conductivity of Au and CQDs, as well as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Au. In particular, the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism has been carefully investigated and proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-53(Fe)
CQDs
Noble metal
Bifunctional photocatalyst
Cr(VI)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) samples
Synthesis of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, (b) MIL-53(Fe) and (c) CQDs.
XRD patterns of (a) MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs samples, (b) MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au with different content of Au.

TEM images of (a-b) MIL-53(Fe), (c-d) CQDs, (e-g) MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, (h) the corresponding size distribution of CQDs in the sample of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, (i-k) TEM images of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag, (l) the corresponding size distribution of Ag in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag, (m-o) TEM images of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd, (p) the corresponding size distribution of Pd in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd, (q-s) TEM images of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au, and the corresponding size distribution of Au in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au.
XPS spectra of samples: (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) Fe 2p, (d) Ag 3d, (e) Pd 3d and (f) Au 4f.
UV–vis DRS spectra of (a) MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs nanocomposites and (b) different Au content of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au.
The valence-band XPS spectrum of MIL-53(Fe).

(a) Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) over MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs samples, (b) kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction under different conditions, (c) effect of Au content of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au on photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction, (d) kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction over MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au samples. Reaction conditions: 20 mg of photocatalyst, 40 mL of 20 mg·L−1 Cr(VI), 5 mg of AO, pH = 4.
The cycle experiment of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au, (b) XRD patterns and (c-d) TEM analyses of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au before and after the reaction.
Photocatalytic treatment of Cr(VI)/dyes co-existed systems. Reaction conditions: 20 mg of photocatalyst, 40 mL of 20 mg·L−1 Cr(VI)/dye, air atmosphere, pH = 7.
(a-b) Light/dark photocurrent response and (c-d) EIS response of samples.
The (a) PL and (b) time-resolved PL decay spectra of MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au.
ESR spectra of various radical adducts.
The flow diagram for the fabrication of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) samples.
A proposed reaction mechanism for photocatalytic treatment of Cr(VI)/dyes co-existed systems over MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au nanocomposites.
The kinetic rate constants of samples towards Cr(VI) photoreduction.
Simultaneous photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of dyes over MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/noble metal co-decorated MIL-53(Fe) as difunctional photocatalysts for the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Province University Key Laboratory of Green Energy and Environment Catalysis, Ningde Normal University, Ningde 352100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Province University Key Laboratory of Green Energy and Environment Catalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Province University Key Laboratory of Green Energy and Environment Catalysis, Ningde Normal University, Ningde 352100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Featured Materials in Biochemical Industry, Ningde Normal University, Ningde 352100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xiamen Ocean Vocational College, Xiamen 361000, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhoujun He and Ruowen Liang are co-first authors of the article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial sewage often contain metal ions and dyes, and it is difficult to efficiently remove them simultaneously. In this work, novel ternary MIL-53(Fe)/carbon quantum dots/noble metal (MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs, MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) nanocomposites have been rationally constructed and successfully fabricated by a facile two-step strategy with self-assembling and in-situ photoreduction. The resulting MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au composite exhibits excellent photoactivity for the Cr(VI) reduction, the reduction ratio is nearly 100% after 20 min of visible light illumination. The rate constant of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au is calculated to be 0.1820 min−1, which is around 7.4, 2.76, 1.77 and 1.47 times higher than that of as-prepared MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd, respectively. More significantly, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au nanocomposite has been proven to serve as bifunctional photocatalyst for simultaneously removing Cr(VI)/dye mixed system pollutants. The superior photoactivity may be due to the enhanced light absorption intensity, the superior electrical conductivity of Au and CQDs, as well as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Au. In particular, the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism has been carefully investigated and proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI) is a kind of common heavy metal ions contaminant existed in industrial sewage, which can cause teratogenic, cancer and mutation to animalsandhuman [1]. Moreover, Cr(VI) and dyes are often mixed together in the wastewater, and it is difficult and costly to remove both types of contaminants thoroughly [2]. Due to its efficiency, mild and no secondary pollution, photocatalysis has been considered as a green technology for one pot removal of mixed pollutants in trade effluent [3,4]. Photocatalysts, as a core factor of photocatalysis, should be investigated in great detail.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are one kind of porous crystalline materials with great merits like tuneable topology, exceptional high surface area and porosity. These advantages make MOFs as favorable functional materials in various fields [5,6]. For photocatalysis, they could not only function as semiconductor-like materials, but also could serve as superior scaffolds to support easily aggregated nanoparticles as the cocatalyst [7–9]. To date, most of MOFs photocatalysts still suffer from low utilization of visible light and rapid recombination of photon-generated carriers. Among various modified approaches, combining MOFs with conductor (metal or metal-like conductor) may offer a new opportunity to overcome the above drawbacks [10,11]. To date, some carbon materials, including graphene (GR), graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanotube (CNT) have been demonstrated as electron reservoirs to trap photoinduced electrons from photocatalysts, resulting in prolonging the lifespan of photogenerated carriers and enhancing the photoactivities toward target reactions [9,12]. It has been also reported that the carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs, with dimension less than 10 nm), a classical kind of carbon materials have attracted increasing interests in photocatalysis owing to their ease of synthesis, inexpensive, environmental-friendly and excellent electron-transfer rates [13,14]. Inspired by these advantages, various semiconductor/CQDs composites (TiO2/CQDs, Fe2O3/CQDs or ZnO/CQDs) have been designed and exhibit enhanced photoactivity [15–17]. It has been well known that MOFs have similar structures and properties with metal oxides. At this point, one important question naturally arise: whether the role of CQDs in MOF-based photocatalytic systems (MOF/CQDs) is similar to previously reported inorganic semiconductor systems?

On the other hand, it is worth noting that the special structure of MOFs-based photocatalysts is conducive to immobilizing noble metal nanoparticles (MNPs) onto MOFs’ framework [11]. MNPs have low Fermi energy level, which can facilitate photogenerated carriers transfer process and improve the photoactivities of the aforementioned binary hybrids. Moreover, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of MNPs could further enhance the solar-energy conversion efficiency of photocatalyst [18,19]. Therefore, it can be expect that the immobilization of MNPs onto the aforementioned MOFs/CQDs composites could further improve their photocatalytic performances. Although there have some reports on the MOFs/CQDs composites, however they always focus on the chemical sensing field, the reports on utilizing such composites in photocatalysis field are rather scarce [20–24]. Especially, preparing novel MOFs/CQDs/MNPs ternary hybrids and further applying to the photocatalytic purification of Cr(VI)/dyes mixed system have not been extensively studied. Therefore, in light of these remarkable advantages of MOFs, CQDs and MNPs, we consider that the combination of these components may be a unique strategy to construct efficient photocatalysts for environment renovation.

Unlike many contemporary highly porous MOFs, MIL-53(Fe) possesses a relative low surface area (10–150 m2/g). However, good physics/chemistry stability and narrow band gap makes it a potential candidate as photocatalyst [25]. In our earlier work, it has been also demonstrated that the sample of MIL-53(Fe) exhibits considerable photoactivity towards Cr(VI) reduction. Inspired by these work, herein we have developed a serious of MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) decorated MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs hybrids. In our case, MIL-53(Fe) could not only serve as host photocatalyst itself, but also as a scaffold to anchor CQDs and MNPs. These as-prepared ternary compounds have been prepared by a simple electrostatic self-assembly/photodeposition method, which is afforded by the substantial electrostatic attractive interaction between positively charged MIL-53(Fe) substrate and negatively charged CQDs in an aqueous solution, followed by a photoreduction strategy for in situ decorating of MNPs on the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs. The as-prepared MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs ternary photocatalysts show excellent photoactivities for Cr(VI) reduction under visible light irradiation. It is also discovered that MOFs/CQDs/Au can serve as difunctional photocatalyst for photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and dyes. To the best of our knowledge, the fabrication MOFs/CQDs/MNPs ternary photocatalysts as well as investigating their photocatalytic performances in the mixed system are pioneered in this work.

Wood based activated carbon, 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC) was obtained from Alfa Aesar China Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). FeCl3·6H2O was supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HNO3, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol and ammonium oxalate (AO) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).
CQDs were synthesized by a HNO3-treatment approach [26]. MIL-53(Fe) was conveniently prepared according to previous reported by Férey et al. [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) samples
ce:section.ce:para: As showed in Scheme 1 , MOFs/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) samples were prepared by a simple two-step electrostatic self-assembly/photodeposition method.

Firstly, CQDs was dissolved in H2O to produce CQDs solution (4 mg·mL−1). Then, a mixture of MIL-53(Fe) (500 mg), CQDs (2.5 mL of CQDs solution, the mass ratio of CQDs is 2%) was added into 30 mL of H2O in a quartz vial. After stirred at 500 r/min for 10 min, the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs suspension was centrifuged and washed until the ion concentration of the supernatant was less than 10 mg/L. The collected sample was vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au)
ce:section.ce:para: The weight addition ratio of Ag, Pd, Au is selected as 2%. Typically, 500 mg of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs was dispersed in 50 mL H2O. Subsequently, 10 mL of ethanol was added to the suspension, and the mixture was stirred magnetically for 60 min. Then, the noble metal precursor aqueous solution (2.10 mL of HAuCl4, 1.57 mL of AgNO3, or 3.45 mL of H2PdCl4, the concentration of noble metal precursor is 10 mg·mL−1) was quickly added to the mixture with the irradiation of 300 W xenon lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm, 60 min). In this process, N2 was bubbled through the solution to ensure the reducing atmosphere. After that, the products were obtained after washing with ethanol and blow-dried with N2. These as-prepared products were named as MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au.

The proportions of Au in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au were adjusted by the mass ratios of r = m(Au): m(MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs), which were set as 1%, 2%, 3% and 4%, respectively. The obtained products were named as MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/1%Au, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/3%Au and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/4%Au according to the different mass ratios.

XRD patterns were collected using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffrractometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra palletized with KBr powder were carried on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 IR spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried on a JEOL model JEM 2010 EX instrument. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were conducted on a Shimadzu UV-2700 instrument. The electron spin response (ESR) was recorded on a JEOL JES-FA200 spectrometer. The ESR signals of radicals spin-trapped by spin-trapped reagent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) under the light spectrum range of λ ≥ 420 nm. The photoluminescence spectra was performed on a CaryEsclipsespectrofluorometer (Cary Esclipse, USA) spectrophotometer. The time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy was recorded on a FLS 980 pectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

A 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter was employed as visible light source. Firstly, 20 mg of photocatalyst and 5 mg ammonium oxalate (AO, as a sacrificial agent) were added into 40 mL of 20 mg·L-1 Cr(VI) solution. The pH of suspension (pH = 4) was adjusted by 1 M HCl solution. Prior to irradiation, the mixed solution was stirred in dark for 60 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. At a certain time interval, 2 mL suspension was taken out and centrifuged. The Cr(VI) content was measured at 540 nm by diphenylcarbazide method with a Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Co.) [28]. For the binary Cr(VI)/dye system (dye = rhodamine B (RhB), malachite green (MG) and methylene blue (MB)), concentration of dye was 20 mg·L-1 and that of Cr(VI) was 20 mg·L-1. The residual concentration of RhB, MG and MB in the supernatant were directly examined at the 554, 617 and 664 nm, respectively.

Fig. 1 displays zeta potential (ξ) value of CQDs at pH = 7. It can be seen that the functional groups (COOH, OH, etc) render CQDs with a negatively charged surface, and a measured zeta potential of −14.8 mV can be observed. On the other hand, in our previous report, when dispersed in neutral water the pure MIL-53(Fe) shows a ξ value of + 4.6 mV, indicating a positively charged surface. Thus, the negatively charged CQDs and positively charged MIL-53(Fe) could spontaneously establish an electrostatic attraction for the self-assembly construction of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs. The formation of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs nanocomposite can be further confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As depicted in Fig. 2 , MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs (curve (a)) has characteristic absorption bands associated with MIL-53(Fe) and CQDs (curve (b) and curve (c)). For instance, in the curve (a) (MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs), the absorption band located at 1378 cm−1 is corresponding to symmetric C-O-C stretching vibrations, which is become stronger than that of MIL-53(Fe) [25]. This is mainly due to the introducing of CQDs, which are functionalized with many oxygen containing functional groups (–COOH, -COOR, etc). Interestingly, compared with CQDs, the C = O stretching vibration peak of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs is shifts from 1658 to 1632 cm−1 [19,29] . It is reasonable because the length of C = O bond in CQDs has been stretched, which could be attribute to the electronic interactions between MIL-53(Fe) and CQDs. These results further confirm the successful hybridization between MIL-53(Fe) and CQDs. It worth noting that other absorption bands of CQDs are negligible in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs. This could be due to: (1) the low loading amount of CQDs (2 wt%) in MIL-53(Fe); (2) terephthalic acid ligands in MIL-53(Fe) present intense absorption bands, which might shield the characteristic bands of CQDs.

As displayed in Fig. 3 (a), the diffraction peaks of as-prepared MIL-53(Fe) is agree well with the previous report, validating the successful synthesis of MIL-53(Fe) [25,27]. After CQDs loading, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs exhibits similar XRD pattern with MIL-53(Fe), validating that the integrity of MIL-53(Fe) is maintained and our electrostatic self-assembly route is mild. However, in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, the diffraction peak of carbon at 2θ angles of 26° is too weak to distinguish, which is attributed to the low content of CQDs (2 wt%). Fig. 3(b) is the XRD patterns of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au samples with different amounts of Au. When the weight ratio of Au is low (1 wt%-2 wt%), there is no obvious characteristic peaks of Au and the XRD patterns are similar with the MIL-53(Fe) and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs. As the weight ratio of Au reach 3 wt%, apparently, a small peak located at 38.1° is appear, which could be ascribed to (111) crystal planes of metallic Au [30].

To ascertain the details of morphology of MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs, TEM images have been recorded and clearly exhibited in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) and (b) show the TEM images of MIL-53(Fe), revealing the as prepared MIL-53(Fe) is composed of uniform nanosheets, and the size of nanoparticles is about 2 μm. As showed in Fig. 4(c) and (d), CQDs are uniform and monodisperse nanoparticles with the size range about 4–6 nm. While grown on MIL-53(Fe), CQDs particles exhibit highly dispersed and decorated on the MIL-53(Fe) (Fig. 4(e)). From Fig. 4(f), the distinct contrast between the MIL-53(Fe) and CQDs further confirmed the successful decorated of CQDs. Fig. 4(g) unambiguously shows HRTEM micrograph of CQDs. The CQDs have lattice spacing of about 0.32 nm, which agrees well with the lattice spacing of (002) plane of graphitic carbon [28]. CQDs size distribution histograms are shown in Fig. 4(h), the average particle size is found to be 4–6 nm, which is almost the same as the pure ones. For the samples of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au), as expected, the presence of Ag, Pd, Au particles are observed on the surface of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and the main morphology of MIL-53(Fe) is almost maintained (Fig. 4(i)-(t)). From Fig. 4(k), (o) and (s), the lattice fringes spacing is measured to be 0.238, 0.224, and 0.235 nm, which could be indexed as the (111) plane of Ag, Pd, and Au NPs, respectively [31–33]. From Fig. 4(i), (p) and (t), the average diameter of Ag, Pd, Au is about 20, 25, and 10 nm, respectively. EDX results clearly present that these ternary hybrids contain the C, O, Fe and Ag/Pd/Au elements (Fig. S1). The weight ratio of noble metal in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs nanocomposites is measured to be 1.890, 1.632, and 1.829 wt% for MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Ag, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Pd and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au, respectively. These indicate that the facile photodeposition pathway is an effective technique for immobilizing MNPs on the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs. Because after the photodeposition treatment, the CQDs are still coated on MIL-53(Fe) and the content of Ag/Pd/Au is very similar to the theoretical one (2 wt%). To sum up, the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs ternary hybrids have been prepared successfully via the high-efficiency electrostatic self-assembly/photodeposition method. For comparison, we have also provide the TEM images of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au samples with different amounts of Au (Fig. S2). It is clearly seen that, with increasing the Au content, the gold nanoparticles gradually become bigger. When the Au content reaches 4%, the gold nanoparticles are severely agglomerated, the maximum diameter of gold nanoparticles is about 100 nm.

The chemical composition and chemical status of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs samples have been characterized by XPS. The typical survey scan of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs samples show the existence of Fe, C, O and noble-metal elements (Fig. 5 (a)), which in good agreement with the EDS analysis. The C1s spectra of samples are divided into three peaks at 284.60, 286.21, and 288.57 eV, corresponding to the carbon-contained function groups (Fig. 5(b)). It should be emphasized that both of H2BDC ligands and CQDs are co-existed in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs. These carbon species like C-C, C = C, C-O and C = O would lead to the signal superposition in the C1s spectra. Therefore, from C1s spectra, it is hard to distinguish the signal of CQDs. As for the MIL-53(Fe), the doublet at 712.05 and 726.01 eV Fe 2p can be assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 5(c)). However, after decorating of CQDs and MNPs, the XPS peaks of Fe 2p3/2 are slightly shifted to a higher binding energy, resulting in a decreased electron density on Fe (Fig. 5(c) and Table 1 ) [22]. This phenomenon also proves that the chemical environment of Fe in the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs is changed due to the strong chemical interactions among MIL-53(Fe), CQDs and MNPs. Fig. 5(d) Fig. 5(e) and Fig. 5(f) show the high resolution XPS spectrum of Ag, Pd and Au in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs, and these well-defined XPS peaks corresponding to Ag0, Pd0 and Au0 [11].

Light adsorption ability is a critical factor for effecting the photoactivity of photocatalysts. Therefore, UV–vis DRS have been recorded to characterize the optical properties of samples (Fig. 6 ). With regard to all the samples, an obvious absorption band in the UV light region ranging from 200 to 400 nm, which is primarily due to absorption induced by ligand-to-metal charge transfer effect of O(II) → Fe(III) [9,25]. It can be seen that the light absorption edge of MIL-53(Fe) is located at around 456 nm. Based on the relation Eg = 1240/λ, the Eg value of MIL-53(Fe) is calculated as 2.72 eV. Due to the small depositing amount of CQDs (2 wt%), only a slightly increased of absorption band is observed over the sample of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs in the range of 400–800 nm [34]. Significantly, we can see that when noble metal has been introduced in the MOFs, the visible light absorption intensity of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs ternary hybrids become more stronger, which is mainly due to the SPR effect of the Ag0, Pd0 and Au0 [33,35]. Noted that CQDs and MNPs only deposits on the surface of MIL-53(Fe), which is unable to change its crystal structure. As a result, after depositing MNPs and CQDs onto the surface of MIL-53(Fe), the absorption edge of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs also do not change but the full spectrum absorption capability is enhanced. Thus, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs show the same bandgap value as MIL-53(Fe). It has been reported that the HOMO of MOFs is approximately equivalent to the valence band positions (VB) of semiconductor. The HOMO position of our samples have been determined by VB-XPS. Fig. 7 revealed that the HOMO positions of MIL-53(Fe) is 2.32 eV. As mentioned above, presents an absorption edge onset around 455 nm, corresponding to the optical bandgap of MIL-53(Fe) evaluated by DRS is 2.72 eV. The position of LOMO is calculated by ELUMO = EHOMO - Eg. Hence, it can be calculated that the relative LOMO position of MIL-53(Fe) is −0.4 eV, which is the same as the LOMO position measured before [25].

The activity of these samples have been investigated using the photoreduction of Cr(VI) under visible light. Our previous study has verified that the Cr(VI) reduction reaction is truly driven by a photocatalytic process [25]. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the concentration of Cr(VI) exhibited almost no any change in the absence of light or photocatalyst. After irradiation for 30 min, the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au is able to photoreduction of Cr(VI), but it only remove 20% of Cr(VI) in the absence of AO. As observed, when addition of a certain amount of AO as hole scavenger, the final removal rate reached to almost 100%, reflecting the important role of hole scavenge. Fig. 8 (a) displays the photocatalytic activity of Cr(VI) reduction over MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs samples. As for pristine MIL-53(Fe), it can merely reduce 51% of Cr(VI) after 30 min of visible light irradiation, which can be attributed to its rapid recombination of charge carriers. By introduction of CQDs, the resulting MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs could enhance the reduction rate in some extent. This is mainly due to the excellent electron mobility of CQDs, which promoting the charge carriers separation at the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs interface. Among all the samples, the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au with average Au particle size of 8 nm exhibits the highest activity towards reduction of Cr(VI). Within visible light illumination for 20 min, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au could completely reduce 20 mg·L-1 of Cr(VI). It should be attributed to the SPR effect of gold nanoparticles, which could not only increasing the light absorption intensity of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au in visible light region, but also promoting an efficient separation of charge carriers. In comparative with the sample of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au, the photocatalytic performance of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag is slightly decreased. This situation has no much improve with the decoration of 2%Pd (removal rate is about 85% within 30 min of irradiation). These probably due the larger particle size and agglomeration effect of Ag or Pd nanoparticles, which has also been proved by the aforementioned TEM measurement. Moreover, the pseudo-first-order equation, was employed to gain insight into the photocatalytic process [28,36], the results are illustrated in Fig. 8(b). The rate constant of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au is calculated to be 0.18197 min−1, which is around 7.4, 2.76, 1.77 and 1.47 times higher than that of the as-prepared MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd, respectively.

It is crucial to control the content of Au nanoparticles in order to achieve an optimal ratio of Au for reduction of Cr(VI). As showed in Fig. 8(c), interestingly, with weight ratio of Au increased, the photoactivity of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au composites first increased and then decreased. With 2 wt% amount of Au, the optimal reduced capacity is received. That is, Cr(VI) could be almost removed completely after 20 min of visible light irradiation. When the weight ratio of Au were increased to 3 wt% or 4 wt%, removal efficiency of Cr(VI) could be decreased. This is due to a higher loading amount of Au would lead to the aggregation effect, which can reduce the reactive sites within the nanocomposites. Such phenomenon has also appears in Au-TiO2, Au-ZnO and Au-TiO2/SiO2 systems [37–39]. The kinetic analyses are depicted in Fig. 7(d), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au exhibits the highest reduction rate constant (0.1820 min−1) (Table 2 ). Combining photoactivity results (Fig. 8) with the aforementioned TEM analyses (Fig. 4 and Fig. S2), it could be revealed that Au nanoparticles with smaller particle size in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au are much more favorable for the photoinduced carrier separation and exhibit preferable photoactivity than that of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs, which decorate with Ag, Pd or agglomerated Au particles with larger particle size. This phenomenon is in good agreement with the previously published papers [11,33].

Furthermore, we have also evaluated the reusability and stability of our sample. As revealed in Fig. 9 (a), photoactivity of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au still retain after five reaction cycles, indicating that the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au shows good catalytic stability during the photocatalytic reaction. Additionally, XRD and TEM results of both the fresh and used MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au reveal that after the reaction no significant change in the crystal structure and microstructure. (Fig. 9(b)-(d)).

With the aim of inspecting the photocatalytic performance of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au nanocomposites toward the synergistic removal of Cr(VI) and dye, we selected Cr(VI)/dye (dye = RhB, MG and MB) as binary system for photocatalytic purification. In the present study, the binary system is simulated using Cr(VI) (20 mg·L-1) and dye (20 mg·L-1) with natural pH (no hole scavenger, air atmosphere). As illustrated in Fig. 10 and Table 3 , under visible light irradiation, Cr(VI) reduction efficiency is not ideal (only 15.3%) and MG is also hard to be removed individually (only 33.6%). However, when MG is mixed with Cr(VI), both of the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) and MG are enhanced (40.2% for Cr(VI) and 100.0% for MG). Similarly, in comparative with the individual RhB system, the removal rate of both Cr(VI) and RhB are significant enhanced in the Cr(VI)/RhB binary system. And this trend is also observed in the Cr(VI)/MB system. The obvious boosted efficiencies of Cr(VI) and dye in binary systems compared to that of single systems could be attributed to the key role of organic dyes, which replaces ammonium oxalate to become a kind of high efficiency hole scavenger, resulting in a higher transfer and separation of photogenerated carriers efficiency in the binary systems. That is, dyes could capture the photogenerated holes and Cr(VI) could combine with electrons, respectively, thereby further attenuate the recombination of photogenerated electrons.

It is observed from Fig. 11 (a) that all of samples exhibit sensitive photocurrent response during repeated over several on/off cycles. The photocurrent density follows the order: MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au > MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Pd > MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Ag > MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs > MIL-53(Fe). As generally accepted, a stronger photocurrent signal means a more effective transfer and separation of photo-excited electrons and holes at the interface of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au. As showed in Fig. 11(b), the photocurrent density of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/Au samples follows the order: MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au > MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/3%Au > MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/4%Au > MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/1%Au, which is also consistent with their photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction activities as discussed above. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) have been performed to further elucidate the charge migration behavior of reaction system. As showed in Fig. 11(c-d), in compared with other samples, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au possesses a smallest arc radius of the Nyquist circle, indicating that the improved charge transfer process in MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au after decorating with CQDs and Au.

In addition, to get further information about the charge transfer and recombination process of our samples, PL spectra have been performed. It can be seen from Fig. 12 (a) that under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the PL spectra of MIL-53(Fe) presents an intense fluorescence emission peak at ca. 450 nm. Following the decoration of the CQDs and Au, the PL intensity decreased dramatically, indicating that the CQDs and Au co-doped on MIL-53(Fe) might be benefit for restraining the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Fig. 12(b) displays the time-resolved PL decay spectra of samples. The average fluorescence lifetime of MIL-53(Fe), MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs and MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au is 0.9653 ns, 0.9295 ns and 0.5996 ns, respectively. The decreasing lifetime clarifies that the CQDs and Au decorating could accelerate light-induced charges separation and transfer more efficiently.

To further investigate the reactive species in the mixed system, ESR spectra have been carried out. Photogenerated hole, as a kind of well-known active specie has been detected. During a photocatalytic reaction, TEMPO often serves as hole probe [40,41]. This is mainly due to their free radicals could be oxidized by the photogenerated holes. As showed in Fig. 13 (a), the ESR signal of TEMPO becomes weaker under visible light irradiation, indicating the production of photoinduced holes. This is due to the redox potential of MB, RhB and MG are about 0.53 V, 1.43 V and 0.90 V vs. NHE, respectively, which is significantly lower than the VB level of MIL-53(Fe) [9,42]. Therefore, a directly hole oxidation process is energetically. As illustrated in Fig. 13(b-c), negligible ESR signals of DMPO/•OH or DMPO/•O2 – are observed under dark condition. Under visible light irradiation, characteristic quartet peaks of the DMPO-•OH adduct can be detected, while the ESR signal of DMPO/•O2 – is relative weak. It is reasonable because the HOMO value of MIL-53(Fe) is 2.32 eV, which is positive than the redox potential of •OH/H2O (2.27 V vs. NHE), thus the holes from the excited MIL-53(Fe) are scavenged by H2O to yield •OH radicals in neutral water (Eq. (1)-(2)) [43]. Furthermore, it should be pointed out that the redox potential of O2/•O2 – is −0.28 V vs. NHE, which is more positive than the LOMO position of MIL-53(Fe). Therefore, molecular oxygen could trap electrons to produce •O2 – [41,43]. Subsequently, •O2 – will combine with H+ to form •OH, which has strong oxidation ability to degrade dyes (Eq. (3)-(6)) [44], leading to a weaker DMPO/•O2 – signal. To sum up, for the Cr(VI)/dyes binary systems, the photogenerated electron plays a pivotal role in the photocatalytic treatment of Cr(VI)/dyes co-existed systems, which mostly combine with Cr(VI) to form Cr(III) (Eq. (7)). As for the photocatalytic oxidation half reaction, the active species such as holes, •OH, and•O2 – radicals directly induced the dye degradation under visible light irradiation (Eq. (8)).
MIL-53(Fe) + hv → e- + h+(1)
h+ + H2O →•OH + H+(2)
e- + O2 → •O2 –(3)
•O2 – + e- + 2H+ → H2O2(4)
•O2 – + H2O2 →•OH + OH– + O2(5)
H2O2 + hv → 2•OH(6)
3e- + Cr(VI) → Cr(III)(7)
h+/•OH /•O2 – + Dyes → intermediates and inorganic compounds(8)

Based on our results, a photocatalytic enhanced mechanism is proposed (Scheme 2 ). Under visible light irradiation, photoinduced carriers are generated on MIL-53(Fe). It has been demonstrated that carbon materials and Au nanoparticles have excellent electron transfer properties. Therefore, for our case, the CQDs and Au nanoparticles could serve as electron acceptors to trap a portion of electrons excited from MIL-53(Fe), thereby hindering the recombination of photoinduced carriers. From Scheme 2, we speculate that the electrons motivated from MIL-53(Fe) are preferably transferred to CQDs at first, subsequently to Au as an ultimate reservoir. It is reasonable because the sequence of electron transfer is judged by the work function of materials, that is, the photogenerated electrons would preferentially transfer to the materials with higher work function [46]. The work function of Au is about 5.1 eV, which is higher than that of CQDs (4.7 eV) [35,45]. Thus, the photogenerated electrons in the MIL-53(Fe) tend to initially transfer to CQDs, and then transfer to the terminal reservoir Au. Once electrons reached Au, they react with Cr(VI) and oxygen in the solution to yield Cr(III) and •O2 –, respectively. Subsequently, •O2 – will combine with H+ to form •OH. One the other hand, holes from the excited MIL-53(Fe) are scavenged by H2O to form more ·OH radicals, which should be responsible for the degradation of dyes. Moreover, the holes also with strong oxidation capacity could oxidize dyes directly.

All these outcomes reveal that the improved photocatalytic performance of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au is attributed to the synergistic effect of MIL-53(Fe), CQDs and Au nanoparticles. Firstly, MIL-53(Fe) serves as a light absorber, increasing the light absorption and generation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Secondly, the electrostatic interaction between MIL-53(Fe) and CQDs should make the composites more stable and then give them a more efficient charge carriers separation. Thirdly, the superior light absorption performance of CQDs and the SPR effect of Au nanoparticles induce the enhanced visible light absorption. Last but not least, the excellent electron separation and transfer capacity of CQDs and Au nanoparticles result in raising charge separation efficiency of the composites, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity. It needs to be emphasized that the MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au with average Au particle size of 10 nm exhibits remarkably improved photocatalytic performance compared with those of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs, which may be due to a more efficient charge transfer capacity and increased visible light absorption intensity of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au. In other words, as the size of Au particles increases, they could act as recombination centers and hamper the photocatalytic performance of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs.

In summary, ternary MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/MNPs (MNPs = Ag, Pd, Au) nanocomposites have been rationally constructed and successfully fabricated through a facile two-step method. Under visible light irradiation, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au results in a 9-fold higher reaction rate than that of MIL-53(Fe) for Cr(VI) photoreduction. More significantly, MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au nanocomposite is proven to serve as bifunctional photocatalyst for simultaneously removing Cr(VI)/dye mixed system pollutants. As evidenced by the experimental result, the superior photocatalytic performance of MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs/2%Au nanocomposite may be attributed to the enhanced light absorption intensity, the electron separation and transfer capacity of Au and CQDs, as well as SPR effect of Au.

Zhoujun He: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ruowen Liang: Visualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Chen Zhou: Software, Validation. Guiyang Yan: Supervision. Ling Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21806085), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019J01016), Incubation Program of Major Research Plan in Ningde Normal University (2019ZDK03) and Natural Science Foundation of Ningde Normal University (2018Z02, 2019T03). Moreover, we are also grateful to the Program of IRTSTFJ for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2020.117725.
